topic.what: Systematic study of 100 consecutive patients undergoing endoscopy demonstrating that curved bacilli (later named Helicobacter pylori) are strongly associated with histological gastritis and peptic ulceration.
topic.how: Gastric biopsies from 100 patients were cultured and histologically examined; organism presence was correlated with gastritis type, peptic ulcer status, and clinical presentation.
topic.why_matters: This seminal paper provided the first large-scale clinical evidence linking a specific bacterium to gastritis and peptic ulcer disease, transforming gastroenterology from a surgical to a medical discipline.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Proportion of patients with gastritis who harbored curved bacilli
characteristics.value: 58 of 100 patients had curved bacilli; almost all had histological gastritis
characteristics.vs_baseline: No prior systematic culture-based prevalence study existed
characteristics.evidence: Prospective endoscopic cohort with culture and histology
characteristics.context: 100 consecutive patients at Royal Perth Hospital gastroscopy clinic

characteristics.what_it_measures: Association between curved bacilli and peptic ulcer disease
characteristics.value: Bacilli found in all 13 duodenal ulcer patients and most gastric ulcer patients
characteristics.vs_baseline: Ulcers previously attributed to acid/stress alone

good_for: Foundation for peptic ulcer treatment revolution
Epidemiological baseline for H. pylori prevalence
not_for: Definitive proof of causation (Koch's postulates not yet fulfilled)

novelty: First successful culture and systematic characterization of the gastric curved bacillus
Demonstrated strong statistical association between the organism and both gastritis and peptic ulcer
Provided epidemiological framework suggesting an infectious etiology for conditions previously considered non-infectious
Introduced the concept that peptic ulcer disease might be curable with antibiotics
open_problems: Koch's postulates not yet fulfilled — causation still unproven
Taxonomy of the organism was uncertain (initially called Campylobacter pyloridis)
